topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a familiar chime that always made my stomach clench. It was a message from Mr. Harrison, my notoriously exacting history teacher. My thumb hovered over the screen, a strange mix of dread and morbid curiosity tightening my chest. I had accidentally clicked on a shared link from a class discussion forum, and now, there it was: his personal Instagram. The carefully constructed facade of academic respect crumbled instantly. Every perfectly composed sentence I’d ever written felt suddenly inadequate. I scrolled through his feed, noticing the curated images of travel and casual gatherings, and a hot flush crept up my neck. Why was he sharing this? Was he mocking me? I quickly minimized the app, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to force a steady breath. The silence of my dorm room suddenly felt heavy, amplifying the frantic rhythm of my pulse.

[story 2]

I stared at my phone screen, the blue light reflecting off my wide, slightly panicked eyes. It was an undeniable fact: Mr. Davies, the man whose critiques always left me feeling hollow, had a public profile. My hands started shaking, the small movements betraying the internal turmoil churning beneath my skin. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to hide under my duvet, to disappear from the world. Every achievement I’d ever celebrated felt tarnished, as if this digital intrusion exposed some fundamental flaw in my character. I reread the caption he’d posted—a vague, philosophical quote—and a sharp, bitter taste rose in my mouth. I couldn't look away. My gaze tracked across his profile, cataloging every shared moment, each perfectly filtered smile. The sheer audacity of it made my jaw ache.

[story 3]

It happened so casually. I was just browsing for a study guide, and there, embedded in a group chat I hadn't intended to join, was a direct link to a private account. When I clicked it, the immediate sensation was a cold wave washing over me, making my limbs feel heavy and sluggish. I leaned back in my chair, my shoulders hunching inward as if bracing for impact. The realization settled in: the person who demanded such flawless output from me was observing me, cataloging me. A wave of profound inadequacy washed through me. I started furiously typing, trying to rationalize the intrusion, to find a logical explanation for why this connection existed. Each word I typed felt clumsy, inadequate, a testament to my own perceived failure to navigate the social landscape correctly.
